skill.name: srs-documentation
skill.description: Software Requirements Specification documentation following IEEE 830 standard. Use when generating formal SRS documents or compiling gathered requirements into structured documentation.
skill.summary: Create IEEE 830-style SRS documents with structured requirements, traceability, and validation checklists.
skill.author: DoubleslashSE

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found them to be false positives in Markdown documentation, diagrams, headings, and placeholders. The skill provides an SRS template and validation guidance, uses only Read, Write, and Glob, and contains no executable scripts, network calls, credential access, or prompt injection attempts.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged Markdown backtick fences and examples as Ruby or shell execution. These locations are documentation examples, section numbering samples, scoring formulas, and diagram placeholders; no executable command is invoked.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is inside Markdown examples or template diagrams. The skill metadata allows only Read, Write, and Glob, and no shell or interpreter invocation is present.

low_findings.title: Requirements Terminology Misclassified as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged common SRS, description, design, section, and placeholder text as weak cryptographic algorithm references. The reviewed locations discuss requirements documentation and do not recommend or execute cryptographic algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are ordinary SRS template content, headings, and placeholders. No line contains cryptographic implementation, weak hash selection, or security bypass instructions.

low_findings.title: System Context Terms Misclassified as Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged system, network, and interface language used in requirements documentation. These references ask authors to document interfaces and dependencies; they do not perform host, network, or service discovery.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is checklist language about requirements completeness and user stories. There is no command, network request, scanner configuration, or target enumeration.

low_findings.title: Template Placeholders Misclassified as Windows SAM Access
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged placeholder table rows in the SRS template as Windows SAM database access. The reviewed lines are generic interface and security requirement placeholders, not filesystem paths or credential database references.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines contain template placeholders such as system and security requirements. They do not reference SAM file paths, registry hives, credentials, or extraction steps.

content.value_statement: Requirements teams often need consistent SRS documents that are complete, testable, and easy to review. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured IEEE 830-style template, writing guidance, and validation checklists.
actual_capabilities: Structures SRS documents with introduction, overall description, specific requirements, and appendices.
Provides templates for functional, non-functional, interface, constraint, and traceability sections.
Guides requirement wording with identifiers, priorities, sources, status, and acceptance criteria.
Includes completeness and quality checklists for SMART, INVEST, traceability, and document review.
Supports consistent requirement IDs for functional requirements, non-functional requirements, and constraints.
limitations: Does not gather stakeholder requirements by itself.
Does not validate requirements against live systems or repositories.
Does not replace legal, regulatory, or domain expert review.
Uses an IEEE 830-style structure rather than a custom organization template.

use_cases.title: Draft a New SRS
use_cases.description: Create a structured first draft from known project goals, users, features, constraints, and acceptance criteria.

use_cases.title: Standardize Requirements
use_cases.description: Convert informal notes into numbered, testable requirements with priorities, sources, and traceability fields.

use_cases.title: Review Specification Quality
use_cases.description: Check an existing requirements document for missing sections, unclear wording, duplicate requirements, and weak acceptance criteria.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic SRS Outline
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prompt for starting a new specification.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an SRS outline for [project name]. Include purpose, scope, users, major features, constraints, assumptions, and appendices.

prompt_templates.title: Turn Notes Into Requirements
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate prompt for organizing raw discovery notes.
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert these project notes into SRS requirements. Use FR and NFR identifiers, priority, source, status, and acceptance criteria: [paste notes].

prompt_templates.title: Review an Existing SRS
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this SRS for completeness, clarity, traceability, and testability. List missing sections and rewrite unclear requirements: [paste document].

prompt_templates.title: Build Traceability and Sign-Off
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a traceability matrix from business objectives to requirements and test cases. Add dependency notes, open issues, and stakeholder sign-off fields: [paste objectives and requirements].

output_examples.input: A product manager asks for an SRS for a customer support portal.
output_examples.output: A structured SRS with product scope, user classes, interface requirements, functional requirements, security needs, performance targets, and appendices.

output_examples.input: A team provides meeting notes with feature requests and constraints.
output_examples.output: A cleaned requirement set with FR and NFR identifiers, priorities, sources, acceptance criteria, and traceability notes.

output_examples.input: An engineering lead shares a draft SRS before stakeholder review.
output_examples.output: A validation report covering missing sections, ambiguous wording, incomplete acceptance criteria, conflicts, and recommended revisions.

best_practices: Provide project goals, stakeholder notes, constraints, and known integrations before generating the SRS.
Keep each requirement atomic, measurable, and traceable to a business objective.
Review the generated SRS with stakeholders before treating it as approved.
anti_patterns: Do not use vague requirements such as fast, scalable, or user-friendly without measurable criteria.
Do not combine several behaviors into one requirement statement.
Do not treat placeholders in the template as completed analysis.

faq.question: What standard does this skill follow?
faq.answer: It follows an IEEE 830-style SRS structure with sections for introduction, product overview, specific requirements, and appendices.

faq.question: Can it write functional and non-functional requirements?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides structures for functional requirements, performance, security, reliability, usability, maintainability, and other requirements.

faq.question: Does it create diagrams?
faq.answer: It includes diagram placeholders and sections for analysis models. You must provide or request the actual diagrams separately.

faq.question: Can it validate an existing SRS?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes checklists for completeness, SMART quality, INVEST user stories, traceability, consistency, and stakeholder readiness.

faq.question: Is it suitable for regulated projects?
faq.answer: It can document regulatory constraints and compliance requirements, but domain experts must verify the final content.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
